does: This paper proposes a novel graph-based approach to understanding sparse autoencoder (SAE) features by representing each feature as a token co-occurrence graph, where nodes are frequent tokens near strong activations and edges capture co-occurrence patterns within local contexts. The authors develop a custom Weisfeiler-Lehman style graph kernel with frequency binning to measure similarity between these structural representations. Applied to features from an SAE trained on GPT-2 Small with a synthetic mixed-domain corpus, the method clusters features into interpretable motif families including punctuation-heavy patterns, language and script clusters, and code-like templates. Importantly, this graph-based clustering recovers structure not found through standard decoder cosine similarity analysis, suggesting that higher-order co-occurrence patterns encode meaningful feature organization beyond individual token statistics.
connects: This work extends mechanistic interpretability research on sparse autoencoders, which have emerged as primary tools for decomposing transformer activations into interpretable monosemantic features. While prior SAE analysis has relied heavily on top-token activation lists and decoder weight vectors, this paper responds to the limitation that these approaches ignore higher-order structural patterns in feature behavior. The graph-based methodology builds on classical graph kernel techniques like the Weisfeiler-Lehman algorithm, adapting them to the novel domain of SAE feature analysis. The work connects to broader lines of research examining polysemanticity and feature interactions in neural networks, as well as to token-level analysis methods in mechanistic interpretability that attempt to ground abstract representations in concrete linguistic units.
enables: This framework opens pathways for deeper structural understanding of SAE feature organizations and could inform improved feature extraction or clustering methods in interpretability research. The graph motif approach suggests that features cluster around meaningful linguistic and semantic patterns that transcend simple token overlap, potentially revealing how transformer circuits organize information. Future work could scale this analysis to larger models and SAEs, explore whether discovered motif families transfer across model architectures or training procedures, or use graph structure to predict feature interactions and combinations. The method might also enable more sophisticated feature discovery by directly optimizing for structural coherence, and could extend to understanding how features compose during model inference, contributing to mechanistic explanations of transformer computation and behavior prediction.
